result:
The first thing that comes to me is the silence. Not an empty silence, but the dense, velvety quiet of a house at 3 a.m., when the world has contracted to the pool of light from a single lamp. It’s in this silence that the mind, unguarded, begins to wander its own back corridors, brushing against forgotten things. A scent—the specific waxy perfume of a certain brand of crayon from childhood—can materialize without warning. The exact feeling of cool linoleum on summer-bare feet. The static-charged thrill of a balloon rubbed on your hair.

We are, all of us, living in a museum of our own making, but we rarely stop to read the plaques. The coffee mug you use every morning: who gave it to you? What conversation did you have with them that day, now lost? The faint scar on your thumb is a archive of a moment of carelessness with a kitchen knife, the flash of panic, the slow surprise of the red beadwell of blood. Our bodies are palimpsests, written over with the stories of stumbles, surgeries, sunburns, and the gentle, persistent press of a loved one’s hand.

I think of the 1000 words as a kind of vessel. Not a trophy case for Big Ideas, but a wooden box into which you can place an assortment of found objects. A smooth stone from a beach in Cornwall. A ticket stub from a film that made you cry for reasons you still don’t understand. A dried-out pen that wrote your first important letter. On their own, they are junk. Together, in this specific container, they become a map to a particular human being.

There is a tyranny to “whatever comes to you.” It sounds so free, but it demands a ruthless honesty. The mind, when given a blank page, often tries to perform. It wants to be profound, witty, cohesive. But what truly comes, if you listen past the noise, are the fragments. The memory of your father’s expression when he was thinking, a look you now catch on your own face in a shop window. The peculiar sadness of finishing a good book and closing the cover, ejecting you from a world that had become more real than your own living room. The absurd, persistent joy of seeing the first daffodil of the year, as if you’ve never seen one before, as if it’s a secret the earth is telling you, again.

We are caught in a strange river of time that flows in only one direction, yet whirlpools constantly pull us back. A song on a supermarket radio can drop you, vertiginously, into the passenger seat of a car you owned fifteen years ago, the smell of rain and wet pavement rushing back. We are time-travelers, but without any control of the destination. The past isn’t even past, as the saying goes; it’s layered under the present like the geological strata under a city. The medieval street plan still dictates the modern traffic flow.

Perhaps this is why we tell stories. To impose a narrative—a beginning, middle, and end—on the chaotic jumble of what comes to us. To take the random scent of crayons and spin it into a tale of a first day of school, of anticipation and new shoes. To make the scar not just a flaw in the skin, but a turning point: “That was the day I learned to be careful.” We are desperate for cause and effect, for meaning, for a plot. But life, in its raw state, is mostly just sensation and emotion, washing over us like weather. The plot is something we construct in hindsight, a comforting fiction.

And what of the future? It never really “comes to you.” It is a phantom, a blank space we fill with dread or hope, both of which are illusions woven from the threads of the past. We project forward our old anxieties, our old wishes, and call it prophecy. The true future is the next breath. The next word. The next turning of the head to see what the cat is doing, knocking a pen off the desk.

Right now, as I write this, a spider is painstakingly constructing a web in the high corner of the window, backlit by the grey dawn. It is a masterpiece of engineering, of patient instinct. It has no idea what I am doing. I am a giant, incomprehensible universe to it, breathing and typing. And I, in turn, feel like a fragile, temporary creature in a cosmos whose scale would shatter my mind to fully comprehend. We are all spiders, spinning our little webs of meaning, hoping to catch something to sustain us, while vast, unfathomable winds blow just outside the frame.

Maybe “whatever comes to you” is simply the present, insisting on itself. The slight ache in the shoulders from sitting too long. The taste of lukewarm tea. The growing lightening of the sky from black to indigo to the washed-out blue of a winter morning. The quiet *click* of the keys. This is it. This is the substance. Not the grand thesis, but the accumulation of moments, attended to.

So if these 1000 words have a purpose, perhaps it is just this: to be a record of a consciousness, for a few minutes, paying attention. Not to the headlines or the drama, but to the whisper of its own existence. A witness to the minor miracle that in a universe of gas giants and silent, spinning galaxies, there is a thing that can feel the warmth of a mug, remember the sound of a lost voice, marvel at a spider’s web, and try, however clumsily, to say: *I was here. This is what it felt like.*

The words are almost gone now. The allotted space is filling up, like a glass under a tap. Soon it will overflow and stop. But the world outside the frame of these sentences goes on. The spider has finished its web. The sky is now pale. A bird begins to sing, a single, questioning note. And whatever comes next, comes next.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
